messages.content: 润色，SCI：In order to solve the problem of difficulty in simulating single particle effects on the ground caused by new technologies such as 3D packaging and flip-packaging of integrated circuits, the high-energy single particle effect research platform on HIRFL came into being. A high-energy single-particle effect research platform not only needs to provide sufficient ion energy and range to test equipment, but more importantly, it must provide precise positioning of single-particle effects. This project plans to use the characteristics of single-chip active silicon pixel detectors such as high position accuracy and good radiation resistance to detect heavy ion hits. On this basis, online heavy ion hit position positioning will be realized, combined with single particle effect events. detection system, thereby realizing an online single-particle effect micron-level positioning system for the high-energy single-particle effect research platform. The position accuracy is expected to reach 5 μm, and the ion hit time resolution reaches 25 ns. The successful development of this system will help improve the single-event effect testing capabilities of the HIRFL high-energy single-event effect research platform and contribute to the development of domestic radiation-resistant integrated circuits.

To address the challenge of replicating single-particle effects in ground-based simulations due to advanced technologies such as 3D integration and flip-chip packaging in integrated circuits, the High-Energy Heavy Ion Research Facility (HIRFL) has developed a dedicated high-energy single-particle effect research platform. This platform is not only designed to deliver the requisite ion energy and penetration depth for thorough equipment testing but also places a premium on the precise localization of single-particle effects.

In line with this project's objectives, the use of monolithic active silicon pixel detectors, known for their exceptional spatial resolution and robust radiation hardness, is planned for the detection of heavy ion strikes. Building on this, the project will implement online localization of heavy ion strike positions, which, when integrated with a single-particle effect event detection system, will culminate in the establishment of a micron-level online localization system specifically tailored for the high-energy single-particle effect research on the HIRFL platform.

This system is anticipated to achieve a positional accuracy of up to 5 micrometers, coupled with an ion strike time resolution of 25 nanoseconds. The successful development and implementation of this system will significantly enhance the single-event effect testing capabilities of the HIRFL platform, thereby making a substantial contribution to the advancement of domestically produced radiation-hardened integrated circuits.

Ultra-black materials with low infrared emissivity are key materials for space detection. In order to avoid stray light interference, the surface of the optical detector of the space telescope will be blackened, which causes heat leakage from the detector surface to the survey focal plane. In order to solve this problem, the Institute of Longitudinal Light of the Chinese Academy of Sciences put forward a clear need: to develop ultra-black materials with low infrared emissivity to meet the selective absorption of visible and infrared light. In response to the above needs, this project proposes an integrated structure of low-infrared emissivity ultra-black materials. Through experiments and theoretical simulations, the controllable preparation and selective light absorption mechanism of low-infrared emissivity ultra-black materials are carried out. The project will achieve controllable preparation of ultra-black materials with low infrared emissivity based on ion tracking technology, study the impact of structural parameters on selective light absorption characteristics, and reveal the mechanism of selective light absorption. The research results of the project will provide key experimental data and theoretical basis for the application of low infrared emissivity ultra-black materials in the field of space telescope detection.

Ultra-black materials with low infrared emissivity play a critical role in space observation technology as they serve to minimize stray light interference and thermal leakage in space telescopes' optical detectors. Addressing this specific need, the Institute of Longitudinal Light of the Chinese Academy of Sciences has called for the development of such materials to selectively absorb both visible and infrared light, thereby optimizing the thermal management and optical performance of space detection systems.

In response to this requirement, the proposed project aims to develop an integrated structure of ultra-black materials with low infrared emissivity. This development process will involve a combination of experimental work and theoretical simulations to gain insights into the controllable fabrication and selective light absorption mechanisms of these materials.

Utilizing ion tracking technology, the project intends to achieve precise control over the preparation of these ultra-black materials, which are characterized by their low emissivity in the infrared spectrum. By investigating the influence of structural parameters on their selective light absorption properties, the project seeks to uncover the underlying mechanisms that enable such selective absorption.

The outcomes of this research will offer valuable experimental data and a theoretical framework that are essential for applying low infrared emissivity ultra-black materials in the field of space telescope detection. Success in this endeavor is expected to significantly advance the capabilities of space telescope technology, improving the clarity and precision of observations made in challenging outer space environments.
不要分段，用于段话

Ultra-black materials with low infrared emissivity are indispensable in space detection as they reduce stray light interference and mitigate heat leakage from optical detector surfaces in space telescopes, thereby maintaining the thermal equilibrium of the survey focal plane. Addressing the challenge posed by the Institute of Longitudinal Light of the Chinese Academy of Sciences, the project strives to develop a cohesive structure of ultra-black materials designed to selectively absorb visible and infrared light. The approach combines experimental validation with theoretical simulations to master the controllable synthesis and elucidate the selective light absorption mechanisms intrinsic to these materials. Capitalizing on ion tracking technology, the project anticipates fine-tuning the fabrication process of ultra-black materials, probing the effects of structural variations on light absorption characteristics, and demystifying the absorption selectivity mechanism. The anticipated results will provide crucial experimental data and a theoretical underpinning for the deployment of low infrared emissivity ultra-black materials in space telescopes, paving the way for enhanced observational capabilities in space exploration.

Ultra-black materials with low infrared emissivity are key materials for space detection. In order to avoid stray light interference, the surface of the optical detector of the space telescope will be blackened, which causes heat leakage from the detector surface to the survey focal plane. In order to solve this problem, the Changchun Institute of Optics and Mechanics of the Chinese Academy of Sciences put forward a clear need: to develop ultra-black materials with low infrared emissivity that can selectively absorb light in the visible and infrared bands. In response to the above needs, this project proposes an integrated structure of low-infrared emissivity ultra-black materials. Through experiments and theoretical simulations, the controllable preparation and selective light absorption mechanism of low-infrared emissivity ultra-black materials are carried out. The project will achieve controllable preparation of ultra-black materials with low infrared emissivity based on ion tracking technology, study the impact of structural parameters on selective light absorption characteristics, and reveal the mechanism of selective light absorption. The research results of the project will provide key experimental data and theoretical basis for the application of low infrared emissivity ultra-black materials in the field of space telescope detection.

The Changchun Institute of Optics and Mechanics of the Chinese Academy of Sciences has identified a critical requirement for the advancement of space detection capabilities: the development of ultra-black materials with low infrared emissivity capable of selectively absorbing light across visible and infrared wavelengths. This innovation is essential to mitigate the issue of stray light interference and the associated thermal leakage from the optical detector surface to the survey focal plane in space telescopes. The project in question proposes the creation of an integrated structure composed of such ultra-black materials, bolstered by a strategic combination of experimental methodologies and theoretical simulations designed to finely tune the preparation process and to probe the mechanisms of selective light absorption. By harnessing ion tracking technology, the project aims to meticulously control the synthesis of these ultra-black materials, evaluate how structural parameters affect their light absorption qualities, and uncover the intricate details of the absorption selectivity process. Upon completion, the project's findings are expected to furnish indispensable experimental data and a robust theoretical foundation that will substantially enhance the application of low infrared emissivity ultra-black materials in space telescope detection systems, offering a significant contribution to the observational precision and thermal management in extraterrestrial research.

In order to solve the problem of difficulty in simulating single particle effects on the ground caused by new technologies such as 3D packaging and flip-packaging of integrated circuits, the high-energy single particle effect research platform on HIRFL came into being. A high-energy single-particle effect research platform not only needs to provide sufficient ion energy and range to test equipment, but more importantly, it must provide precise positioning of single-particle effects. This project plans to use the characteristics of single-chip active silicon pixel detectors such as high position accuracy and good radiation resistance to detect heavy ion hits. On this basis, online heavy ion hit position positioning will be realized, combined with single particle effect events. detection system, thereby realizing an online single-particle effect micron-level positioning system for the high-energy single-particle effect research platform. The position accuracy is expected to reach 5 μm, and the ion hit time resolution reaches 25 ns. The successful development of this system will help improve the single-event effect testing capabilities of the HIRFL high-energy single-event effect research platform and contribute to the development of domestic radiation-resistant integrated circuits.

In response to the challenges faced in emulating single particle effects on Earth due to advancements in integrated circuit technologies like 3D packaging and flip-chip packaging, the High Intensity heavy ion Accelerator Facility (HIRFL) has established a high-energy single particle effect research platform. Essential to this platform is not only the provision of adequate ion energy and penetration capabilities for testing but, crucially, the precise localization of single particle effects. The present project aims to leverage the high positional accuracy and robust radiation tolerance of monolithic active silicon pixel detectors to track heavy ion strikes. Building on this technology, the project intends to implement online positioning for heavy ion impacts, in collaboration with a system that detects single particle effect events, culminating in a real-time micron-level positioning capability within the high-energy single particle research platform. With an anticipated positioning accuracy of 5 micrometers and a timing resolution for ion hits of 25 nanoseconds, the successful development of such a system promises to significantly enhance the testing of single-event effects at HIRFL, while also propelling the progress of domestically produced radiation-hardened integrated circuits.
不要改变原文意思

Addressing the challenge of replicating single particle effects on the ground, complicated by innovative integrated circuit technologies like 3D and flip-chip packaging, the High Intensity heavy ion Accelerator Facility (HIRFL) has established a specialized high-energy single particle effect research platform. This sophisticated platform is designed to provide not only the necessary ion energy and reach to thoroughly test equipment but also to accurately pinpoint single particle effects. The project proposes to utilize the exceptional position accuracy and radiation resistance of single-chip active silicon pixel detectors to detect heavy ion strikes. Based on this capability, the initiative will enable online heavy ion hit position location in conjunction with a detection system for single particle effect events, culminating in an online single particle effect positioning system with micron-level accuracy for the high-energy single particle effect research platform. The anticipated positioning precision is expected to reach 5 micrometers, with an ion hit time resolution of 25 nanoseconds. The fruition of this system will bolster the testing prowess for single-event effects at HIRFL and drive forward the development of domestically engineered radiation-resistant integrated circuits.
使用SCI写作标准进行润色

To address the complexities of simulating single-particle effects on terrestrial platforms due to the advent of advanced integrated circuit technologies, such as 3D and flip-chip packaging, the High Intensity heavy-ion Accelerator Facility (HIRFL) has initiated the development of a cutting-edge high-energy single-particle effect research platform. This platform is engineered to furnish not only sufficient ion energy and trajectory depth requisite for comprehensive equipment validation but also to precisely localize single-particle interactions. The project is charted to harness the remarkable spatial resolution and inherent radiation resilience of single-chip active silicon pixel detectors for the detection of heavy ion strikes. Building upon this technology, it endeavors to integrate an online heavy ion hit localization feature with a single-particle effect event detection system, ultimately establishing a real-time, micron-level positioning facility within the high-energy single particle effect research apparatus. With positioning accuracy projected to be as fine as 5 micrometers and ion hit time resolution up to 25 nanoseconds, the implementation of this system stands to significantly enhance HIRFL's capability to assess single-event effects and will propel the advancement of indigenous radiation-tolerant integrated circuitry.
HIRFL的全称是兰州重离子加速器研究装置

Facing the challenge of ground-based simulations of single-particle effects, which are complicated by state-of-the-art integrated circuit technologies such as 3D packaging and flip-chip packaging, the Heavy Ion Research Facility in Lanzhou (HIRFL) has pioneered a high-energy single-particle effect research platform. This platform is meticulously engineered to deliver not only the requisite ion energy and range necessary for the rigorous testing of equipment but also to achieve precise localization of single-particle interactions. Within the scope of the project, the unparalleled positional accuracy and exceptional radiation endurance of single-chip active silicon pixel detectors are to be utilized for the detection of heavy ion strikes. Expanding on this capability, the project aims to facilitate online heavy ion hit localization in synergy with a detection system tailored for single-particle effect events, thereby creating a real-time, micron-level positioning system on the HIRFL high-energy single-particle effect research platform. Anticipated to reach a positioning accuracy of 5 micrometers and an ion hit time resolution of 25 nanoseconds, the successful development of this system is expected to substantially enhance the single-event effect testing capabilities at HIRFL and contribute significatively to the evolution of China's radiation-resistant integrated circuits.
Controllable preparation and selective light absorption mechanism of ultra-black materials with low infrared emissivity

The development of ultra-black materials with low infrared emissivity is a critical area of research for applications such as space detection, where they are used to minimize stray light interference and improve thermal management. The objective is to create materials that can selectively absorb light in the visible to infrared spectrum while minimizing the emission of infrared radiation, which is pivotal for maintaining the sensitivity and accuracy of optical systems in space telescopes and other sensitive detection instruments.

Controllable preparation refers to the precise fabrication process by which these materials are synthesized. This involves tailoring the materials' microstructure and surface composition to achieve the desired absorption characteristics. Techniques such as ion beam etching, chemical vapor deposition, or electrochemical processes can be adapted to manipulate the surface at the microscopic or nanoscopic scale, optimizing the texture and geometry for maximal light absorption.

The selective light absorption mechanism in these ultra-black materials typically involves the use of nanostructures that trap photons and convert them into heat. This can be achieved through the implementation of nano-scale surface textures that encourage multiple internal reflections, effectively trapping light within the material until it is absorbed. The geometry, size, and distribution of these nanostructures are engineered to target specific wavelengths of light, which requires a detailed understanding of the interaction between electromagnetic radiation and the material's surface.

To achieve low infrared emissivity, the materials may be designed to reflect infrared radiation or to have very poor re-radiation efficiency at infrared wavelengths. This ensures that any heat absorbed from the visible spectrum is not re-emitted in the infrared, preventing the material from contributing to thermal noise in sensitive detectors.

Research into these materials entails both theoretical simulations to model and predict the behaviors of light absorption and experimental work to synthesize and test the actual materials. The synergy between simulation and experimentation allows for the fine-tuning of the materials' properties and the development of ultra-black materials with highly selective absorption and low emissivity, tailor-made for advanced applications in space detection and beyond.
翻译：低红外发射率超黑材料的可控制备及选择性光吸收机理研究
Research on the Controllable Preparation and Selective Light Absorption Mechanism of Ultra-Black Materials with Low Infrared Emissivity
不够好

"探究低红外发射率超黑材料的可控制备与选择性光吸收机制" 

This translation conveys a more academic and research-oriented tone, suitable for the context of a scientific study or project title.
重新翻译
"研究低红外辐射率超黑材料的可控合成及其选择性光吸收机理"
翻译：低红外发射率超黑材料的可控制备及选择性光吸收机理研究
Investigation of Controllable Fabrication and Selective Light Absorption Mechanism of Ultra-Black Materials with Low Infrared Emissivity

润色：In order to address the complexities of simulating single event effects (SEEs) on terrestrial platforms due to the advent of advanced integrated circuit technologies, such as 3D and flip-chip packaging, the High-Energy Single-Event Effects Research Platform at the Heavy Ion Research Facility at Lanzhou (HIRFL) has been established. This platform is tasked not only with providing adequate ion energy and penetration depth for testing devices but, more critically, with the precise localization of SEEs. This project proposes to leverage the high positional accuracy and excellent radiation tolerance of monolithic active silicon pixel detectors to discern the impact points of heavy ions. Building on this capability, the project aims to achieve real-time positioning of heavy ion strikes and, by integrating with a single particle effect event detection system, an online SEEs precision localization system for the High-Energy Single-Event Effects Research Platform will be realized. The successful development of this system will enhance the SEEs testing capability of the High-Energy Single-Event Effects Research Platform at HIRFL and contribute to the advancement of radiation-hardened integrated circuits in China

To tackle the challenges in replicating single event effects (SEEs) on Earth, brought forth by innovative integrated circuit technologies such as 3D and flip-chip packaging, the High-Energy Single-Event Effects Research Platform at the Heavy Ion Research Facility in Lanzhou (HIRFL) was conceived. This sophisticated platform endeavors to deliver not merely sufficient ion energy and range for thorough device testing but also to accurately pinpoint the SEEs. The current project capitalizes on the exceptional spatial resolution and robust radiation resistance of monolithic active silicon pixel detectors for detecting the incidence of heavy ions. Advancing this feature, the system is designed to facilitate instantaneous localizing of heavy ion strikes and, when synergized with an event detection system for single particle effects, an online SEEs precision localization mechanism will materialize for the HIRFL's High-Energy Single-Event Effects Research Platform. The fruition of this groundbreaking system promises to significantly bolster the SEEs testing functions of HIRFL and drive the evolution of radiation-hardened integrated circuits within China's technological landscape.
请从产业需求和科研贡献两方面阐述单粒子效应精准定位在集成电路中的重要性

产业需求方面：

集成电路（IC）是现代科技产业的核心，随着技术的迅猛发展，电路的小型化和复杂性不断提升，3D封装和翻转芯片等尖端技术的应用也日渐增多。然而，随着电路尺寸的缩小，单粒子效应（Single-Event Effects，SEEs）对集成电路的影响愈发显著，即使是微小的辐射粒子也足以引发故障或者性能降低。这在航天、军事和高端电子设备等领域尤为关键，因为在这些领域中，电路必须在高辐射环境下保持可靠运行。因此，从产业的角度看，发展能够精准定位单粒子效应的技术对于优化集成电路设计，提高其抗辐射能力，确保设备性能和稳定性至关重要。

科研贡献方面：

在科研层面，单粒子效应精准定位技术的进步有多方面的贡献。首先，它使研究者能够准确识别和分析在高能粒子撞击过程中发生的微观物理现象，这对深入理解SEEs本质、推进物理模型的建立和精细化改进具有重要作用。其次，通过精准SEEs定位，研究者可以评估不同集成电路设计对抗辐射能力的效果，促进更安全、更可靠的电路布局和制造技术的开发。此外，这一技术进步还可能为其他领域，如材料科学、纳米技术和电子工程等带来新的理解和应用机会。总而言之，单粒子效应的精准定位提供了宝贵的实验数据和理论支撑，对于推动抗辐射集成电路的科研和产业发展都具有深远的影响。
分别使用一句话说明

产业需求方面：精准定位单粒子效应对于发展高可靠性、抗辐射的集成电路至关重要，以确保在高辐射环境中的航天、军事及智能设备的稳定性和安全性。

科研贡献方面：单粒子效应的精准定位技术在揭示辐射粒子与微电路互动的微观机理、推动抗辐射IC设计优化及促进相关交叉学科研究中起到了关键作用。
重写：图3所示的是单粒子效应微米级定位系统原理框图，硅像素探测器与被测器件之间进行高精度固定。

Figure 3 presents the schematic diagram of the micron-level positioning system for single particle effects, where the silicon pixel detector is fixed with high precision in relation to the device under test.
中文
图3展示了单粒子效应微米级定位系统的原理框图，其中硅像素探测器与待测设备之间实现了高精度的固定配合。
（1）如何实现硅像素探测器与被测器件之间的相对位置高精度固定，提升系统的定位精度；（2）硅像素探测器产生的数据量庞大，如何保证大数量传输的稳定传输，提升系统的鲁棒性；（3）如何实现单粒子事件在线检测和定位；

为了提升硅像素探测器与被测器件之间的定位精度，可以采用精密的机械定位装置、亚微米级的位移平台，以及先进的视觉对准系统，确保两者在空间上的高精准对齐。对于硅像素探测器产生的庞大数据量，可以通过高速数据采集系统和优化的数据压缩算法来确保信息的高效处理和稳定传输，以此提高系统的鲁棒性。至于单粒子事件的在线检测和定位，则需集成高速电子学组件和实时数据处理软件，利用专门算法实时分析探测器的响应，从而实现单粒子事件的即时识别和准确定位。
为完成此在线单粒子效应微米级定位系统的应用，还有许多挑战需要克服，其中最关键的几个问题是：（1）如何实现硅像素探测器与被测器件之间的相对位置高精度固定，提升系统的定位精度；（2）硅像素探测器产生的数据量庞大，如何保证大数量传输的稳定传输，提升系统的鲁棒性；（3）如何实现单粒子事件在线检测和定位；

To fully operationalize this online micron-level single particle effect positioning system, numerous challenges must be surmounted, including the critical issues of: (1) achieving highly precise relative positioning between silicon pixel detectors and the devices under test to enhance the system's locational accuracy; (2) handling the vast volume of data generated by silicon pixel detectors and ensuring stable transmission of large datasets to boost system robustness; (3) implementing online detection and precise positioning of single particle events.
使用中文，将每一点扩展一下

要实现在线单粒子效应微米级定位系统的实际应用，需要解决若干技术挑战：

1. 高精度固定位置：核心问题在于如何设计一个既可靠又灵活的接口，使硅像素探测器与被测器件之间的相对位置能达到微米甚至纳米级的精度固定。这可能涉及到复杂的机械系统设计，精准的自动控制反馈机制，以及用于多轴精细调整的微操作技术，确保长时间稳定性与可重复性。

2. 数据处理与传输：硅像素探测器能够产生大量高分辨率的数据，因此必须开发高效的数据收集、处理和传输系统。这包括使用先进的电子学设计来减少数据体积、提高处理速率，以及采用高带宽、低延迟的通信技术保证数据在不同设备之间的稳定、快速传输。

3. 实时检测与定位技术：单粒子事件的在线检测与定位要求系统不仅能够捕捉到粒子的撞击事件，还要快速准确地判定撞击位置。这意味着需要结合复杂的信号处理算法和高速的计算平台，实施实时数据分析，以准确捕捉并定位单个粒子带来的影响。

总体而言，克服这些挑战需要多学科领域的密切协作，包括精密工程设计、高性能计算处理、以及智能算法开发等方面的创新进展。

本项目将设计如图5所示的高性能重离子在线定位与单粒子效应检测系统，主要实现以下功能：（1）硅像素探测器数据读出；（2）重离子击中位置检测；（3）被测器件单粒子效应检测、记录和闩锁保护。在本系统中提供多个通用连接器，用户研制的被测器件通过通用连接器连接到系统中进行供电和与系统进行数据交换。高性能数据获取系统研制过程中有以下几个关键内容需要研究：
（1）	电子学系统可复用性。为后续系统升级需要，电子学系统要进行通用接口设计、模块化组件构建等工作，降低后续成本并提高资源利用率。
（2）	大数据量稳定传输。为满足国产硅像素探测器大数量的传输，定位系统的研制过程中采用具有高性能、高带宽的FPGA作为传输处理器，使用动态存储器DDR4作为数据缓存介质，
（3）	高性能FPGA逻辑设计。FPGA逻辑设计是整个高性能数据获取系统的核心工作，要满足如下功能：（1）实现重离子

击中位置快速高精度的定位；（2）处理硅像素探测器读出的大规模数据量；（3）对被测器件发生的单粒子效应进行及时检测，并作出快速响应进行数据记录和闩锁保护，以防止数据损失和设备损害。通过设计高性能的FPGA逻辑，系统将能够进行高效率的数据处理，确保大量数据的实时处理与稳定流转。此外，系统中将集成智能算法来优化数据流的处理流程与策略，以最小化处理延迟并提高整个系统的数据吞吐能力和鲁棒性。通过这些创新的设计和技术实现，本项目旨在为用户提供一个可靠、灵活且高效的测试平台，用于高性能重离子在线定位与单粒子效应的检测和分析。
重写

本项目旨在构建图5展示的高性能重离子在线定位与单粒子效应检测系统，并确保其实现以下关键功能：（1）读取硅像素探测器数据；（2）精确检测重离子击中位置；（3）对被测器件发生的单粒子效应进行监测、记录以及实施闩锁保护以防数据和设备损坏。系统配备多个通用接口以便用户连接自研发的器件，进行供电并与系统交换数据。高性能数据采集系统的开发涉及几个关键领域，包括（1）电子学系统的可复用性，需设计通用接口并构建模块化组件，以降低未来升级成本并提高效率；（2）大数据量的稳定传输，采用高带宽的FPGA作为处理器，并利用DDR4动态存储作数据缓存进行数据传输；（3）高性能FPGA逻辑设计，需要满足快速定位、处理大容量数据以及即时检测单粒子效应并快速响应的需求。通过这些设计，本项目将向用户提供一个高效、可靠的平台，以支持高精度的重离子在线定位和单粒子效应的检测及分析。
